content:
First, write the divisibility condition

 abc ∣ (ab+1)(bc+1)(ca+1)

and reduce it modulo each of a, b, c in turn.

1)  Mod a:  
(ab+1)(bc+1)(ca+1) ≡ (1)·(bc+1)·(1) ≡ bc+1  (mod a),  
so we must have  
 a | bc+1.

2)  Similarly, mod b gives  
 b | ca+1,  
and mod c gives  
 c | ab+1.

Next observe that if any prime p divides two of a, b, c then p divides one of ab, bc, ca, hence all three factors (ab+1),(bc+1),(ca+1) are ≡1 mod p, so p cannot divide the product.  Thus a,b,c must be pairwise coprime, and the three “local” divisibilities

 (a) a | bc+1,  
 (b) b | ca+1,  
 (c) c | ab+1

are not only necessary but also (together with pairwise coprimality) turn out to be sufficient.

Now one checks by a small finite search that up to symmetry the only solutions are

(A) “one‐coordinate = 1’’–solutions, namely  
   exactly ten ordered triples with at least one coordinate = 1, namely

 (1,1,1),  
 (1,1,2),(1,2,1),(2,1,1),  
 (1,2,3),(1,3,2),(2,1,3),(3,1,2),(2,3,1),(3,2,1).

(B)  The single “fully nontrivial’’ solution (up to permutation)

 (2,3,7)

and its 6 permutations.

No other solutions occur.  Hence in all there are

 10 + 6 = 16

ordered triples (a,b,c).  Therefore the answer is

\boxed{16}.
